sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A biochemistry student prepares a phosphate buffer at pH 7.4 using a mixture of NaH₂PO₄ (monobasic sodium phosphate, the acid form) and Na₂HPO₄ (dibasic sodium phosphate, the conjugate base form). The pKa for the second dissociation of phosphoric acid (H₂PO₄⁻ ⇌ HPO₄²⁻ + H⁺) is 7.2. The student uses the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation to determine the appropriate ratio of conjugate base to acid.
questions.stem: To prepare the buffer at pH 7.4, the student needs a [HPO₄²⁻]/[H₂PO₄⁻] ratio of approximately:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1.6, because pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]), so 7.4 = 7.2 + log(x), giving log(x) = 0.2, x ≈ 1.6

choices.label: B
choices.text: 0.63, because the acid form must be in excess when pH > pKa

choices.label: C
choices.text: 1.0, because all buffers require equal concentrations of acid and conjugate base

choices.label: D
choices.text: 10, because the pH is 0.2 units above the pKa, and the ratio must be 10^(pH-pKa) = 10^0.2 ≈ 10

explanations.A: Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]). Substituting: 7.4 = 7.2 + log([HPO₄²⁻]/[H₂PO₄⁻]). log(ratio) = 0.2. Ratio = 10^0.2 ≈ 1.58 ≈ 1.6. Since pH > pKa, the conjugate base is in slight excess over the acid form, which is consistent with the general rule that the base form predominates when pH > pKa.
explanations.B: 0.63 would mean the acid form predominates, which occurs when pH < pKa. Here pH (7.4) > pKa (7.2), so the base form predominates and the ratio must be > 1. Additionally, 0.63 ≈ 10^(-0.2), which is the inverse of the correct ratio.
explanations.C: Equal concentrations ([A⁻]/[HA] = 1) occur only when pH = pKa. Since pH ≠ pKa here, the ratio is not 1.0. The ratio equals 1.0 only at the half-equivalence point of a titration or when pH exactly equals pKa.
explanations.D: 10^0.2 ≈ 1.58, not 10. The exponent is 0.2, not 1. 10^1 = 10 would require pH − pKa = 1.0 (i.e., pH = 8.2), not 0.2.

questions.stem: Phosphate buffer is effective at pH 7.4 because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: all triprotic acids make effective buffers at any pH

choices.label: B
choices.text: phosphate is a strong acid that completely dissociates at physiological pH

choices.label: C
choices.text: the buffer works by neutralizing both strong acids and strong bases added to the solution

choices.label: D
choices.text: the pKa (7.2) is within approximately 1 pH unit of the target pH, which is the range where buffer capacity is greatest

explanations.A: Triprotic acids have three pKa values, and they are effective buffers only near each individual pKa. Phosphoric acid buffers near pH 2.1 (pKa1), 7.2 (pKa2), and 12.4 (pKa3) — not at arbitrary pH values.
explanations.B: Phosphoric acid is a weak triprotic acid, not a strong acid. Its buffering capacity depends on the equilibrium between its conjugate acid-base pairs, not on complete dissociation. Strong acids (HCl, HNO₃) dissociate completely and do not form buffers.
explanations.C: While it is true that buffers neutralize both added acids and bases (the acid form neutralizes added base, and the conjugate base form neutralizes added acid), this describes the general mechanism of buffering, not why phosphate specifically is effective at pH 7.4. The key is the proximity of pKa2 to the target pH.
explanations.D: A buffer is most effective when pH is within ±1 unit of the pKa of the buffering species. At pH 7.4, the second pKa of phosphate (7.2) falls well within this range (7.2 ± 1 = 6.2–8.2). Within this range, both the acid form (H₂PO₄⁻) and conjugate base form (HPO₄²⁻) are present in significant concentrations, allowing the buffer to neutralize added acid or base.

questions.stem: If the student adds 0.01 mol of HCl to 1 liter of this phosphate buffer (total buffer concentration 0.1 M), the pH will:

choices.label: A
choices.text: decrease slightly because the conjugate base (HPO₄²⁻) will neutralize the added H⁺, converting to H₂PO₄⁻, with the new ratio slightly shifting toward the acid form

choices.label: B
choices.text: decrease dramatically to approximately pH 2, because HCl is a strong acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: increase because adding acid to a buffer always raises the pH

choices.label: D
choices.text: remain exactly at 7.4 because buffers prevent any pH change regardless of how much acid is added

explanations.A: The added H⁺ from HCl reacts with HPO₄²⁻ (conjugate base): HPO₄²⁻ + H⁺ → H₂PO₄⁻. This converts some base form to acid form, decreasing the [A⁻]/[HA] ratio. By Henderson-Hasselbalch, a decreased ratio means a lower pH. With the starting ratio of ~1.6 ([base] ≈ 0.061 M, [acid] ≈ 0.039 M) and 0.01 mol HCl added to 1 L, the new ratio becomes ~1.05, giving pH ≈ 7.2 + log(1.05) ≈ 7.22. The pH drops from ~7.40 to ~7.22 — a noticeable but moderate change that keeps the solution well within the buffer's effective range.
explanations.B: In a buffered solution, the conjugate base absorbs the added H⁺. With 0.1 M buffer (0.1 mol total) and only 0.01 mol HCl added, the buffer has abundant capacity to neutralize the acid. The pH will decrease slightly, not drop to 2. A drop to pH 2 would occur only in unbuffered water.
explanations.C: Adding acid to any solution decreases pH (or keeps it stable in a buffer), never increases it.
explanations.D: Buffers resist pH change but do not prevent it entirely. The pH will shift slightly toward lower values as the ratio of base to acid decreases. Buffers have finite capacity — if enough acid is added to consume all the conjugate base, the buffer is overwhelmed and pH drops sharply.

questions.passage: A student titrates 50 mL of 0.10 M acetic acid (CH₃COOH, pKa = 4.76) with 0.10 M NaOH. The student monitors pH throughout the titration and plots a titration curve. Key points on the curve include: the initial pH before any NaOH is added, the half-equivalence point (where half the acetic acid has been neutralized), the equivalence point (where all acetic acid has been neutralized), and the post-equivalence region.
questions.stem: At the half-equivalence point, the pH of the solution equals:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 4.76, because at the half-equivalence point [CH₃COOH] = [CH₃COO⁻], and pH = pKa when the acid and conjugate base are in equal concentrations

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2.38, because the half-equivalence point pH equals pKa/2

choices.label: C
choices.text: 9.52, because the half-equivalence point pH equals 2 × pKa

choices.label: D
choices.text: 7.0, because the half-equivalence point is always neutral

explanations.A: At the half-equivalence point, exactly half the original weak acid has been converted to its conjugate base. Therefore [CH₃COOH] = [CH₃COO⁻], and the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation gives pH = pKa + log(1) = pKa + 0 = pKa = 4.76. This is a fundamental relationship: the half-equivalence point is where pH = pKa, and it is also the point of maximum buffer capacity.
explanations.B: pKa/2 has no physical significance in acid-base chemistry. The half-equivalence point pH is pKa, not pKa/2.
explanations.C: There is no principle that the half-equivalence point pH equals 2 × pKa. This calculation (2 × 4.76 = 9.52) would give a pH in the basic range, which is incorrect for a solution containing equal amounts of weak acid and its conjugate base.
explanations.D: pH 7.0 occurs only when [H⁺] = [OH⁻], which is true in pure water or in specific buffer/salt solutions. The half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration is not neutral.

questions.stem: At the equivalence point, all acetic acid has been converted to sodium acetate (CH₃COONa). The pH at the equivalence point is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: exactly 7.0, because equal moles of acid and base have reacted

choices.label: B
choices.text: below 7.0, because acetate ion is a strong acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: above 7.0, because acetate ion (the conjugate base of a weak acid) hydrolyzes in water, producing OH⁻ and raising the pH

choices.label: D
choices.text: equal to the pKa (4.76), because the equivalence point always equals the pKa

explanations.A: pH 7.0 at the equivalence point occurs only for strong acid-strong base titrations, where the salt produced does not hydrolyze. In a weak acid-strong base titration, the conjugate base (acetate) hydrolyzes, making the solution basic. Equal moles of acid and base have reacted, but the product is not neutral.
explanations.B: Acetate ion is a weak base (the conjugate base of weak acetic acid), not a strong acid. It accepts protons from water: CH₃COO⁻ + H₂O ⇌ CH₃COOH + OH⁻, producing hydroxide and raising the pH above 7.
explanations.C: At the equivalence point, all CH₃COOH has been converted to CH₃COO⁻ (acetate). Acetate is the conjugate base of a weak acid, so it undergoes hydrolysis: CH₃COO⁻ + H₂O ⇌ CH₃COOH + OH⁻. This produces OH⁻, making the solution basic (pH > 7). The equivalence point pH for a weak acid-strong base titration is always above 7. For 0.10 M acetic acid with pKa = 4.76, the equivalence point pH is approximately 8.7.
explanations.D: pH = pKa at the half-equivalence point, not the equivalence point. At the equivalence point, no buffer exists — all the acid has been converted to its conjugate base.

questions.stem: The student needs to choose an appropriate indicator for this titration. The indicator should change color near the equivalence point (~pH 8.7). Which indicator would be most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Methyl orange (color change range pH 3.1–4.4)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Congo red (color change range pH 3.0–5.0)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Litmus (color change range pH 5.0–8.0)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phenolphthalein (color change range pH 8.2–10.0)

explanations.A: Methyl orange changes color in the pH 3–4 range, which is far below the equivalence point pH of ~8.7. It would change color long before the equivalence point is reached, giving an inaccurate endpoint.
explanations.B: Congo red changes color in the pH 3–5 range, far below the equivalence point of this weak acid–strong base titration (~pH 8.7). It would signal the endpoint too early and would not accurately identify the equivalence point.
explanations.C: Litmus changes color near pH 7, which is below the equivalence point. It would change color before the equivalence point is reached, underestimating the amount of NaOH needed.
explanations.D: Phenolphthalein changes color in the pH 8.2–10.0 range, which includes the equivalence point pH of ~8.7. This makes it an appropriate indicator for a weak acid-strong base titration because its transition range overlaps with the steep portion of the titration curve near the equivalence point.

questions.stem: A solution of 0.10 M HCl has a pH of 1.0. This is because HCl is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a weak acid that partially dissociates, and the equilibrium happens to produce [H⁺] = 0.10 M

choices.label: B
choices.text: a strong base that produces hydroxide ions at a concentration of 0.10 M

choices.label: C
choices.text: a buffer that maintains pH at 1.0 regardless of dilution

choices.label: D
choices.text: a strong acid that completely dissociates in water, so [H⁺] = 0.10 M and pH = −log(0.10) = 1.0

explanations.A: HCl is a strong acid, not a weak acid. It dissociates completely (>99.9%) in aqueous solution: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻. There is no equilibrium to consider — the dissociation goes to completion.
explanations.B: HCl is a strong acid, not a base. It donates protons (H⁺) to water, not hydroxide ions.
explanations.C: HCl is not a buffer. A buffer requires a weak acid and its conjugate base in solution. HCl is a strong acid that does not establish an equilibrium with its conjugate base (Cl⁻ is a spectator ion with negligible basicity). Diluting HCl changes its pH.
explanations.D: Strong acids like HCl, HBr, HI, HNO₃, H₂SO₄ (first dissociation), and HClO₄ dissociate completely in water. For 0.10 M HCl: [H⁺] = 0.10 M. pH = −log[H⁺] = −log(0.10) = −(−1) = 1.0. No Ka calculation is needed because the dissociation is complete.

questions.stem: A 0.10 M solution of NH₃ (Kb = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵) has a pH closest to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 11.1, because NH₃ is a weak base and [OH⁻] = √(Kb × C) = √(1.8 × 10⁻⁵ × 0.10) ≈ 1.3 × 10⁻³ M

choices.label: B
choices.text: 7.0, because all nitrogen-containing compounds are neutral in water

choices.label: C
choices.text: 2.9, because NH₃ is a weak acid with pKa = 4.74

choices.label: D
choices.text: 13.0, because NH₃ is a strong base that completely dissociates

explanations.A: NH₃ is a weak base: NH₃ + H₂O ⇌ NH₄⁺ + OH⁻. For weak base equilibrium: [OH⁻] = √(Kb × C) = √(1.8 × 10⁻⁵ × 0.10) = √(1.8 × 10⁻⁶) ≈ 1.34 × 10⁻³ M. pOH = −log(1.34 × 10⁻³) ≈ 2.87. pH = 14 − pOH = 14 − 2.87 = 11.13 ≈ 11.1. The solution is basic but not strongly so — consistent with a weak base.
explanations.B: Nitrogen-containing compounds are not uniformly neutral. NH₃ is a well-known weak base that accepts protons from water. Its aqueous solutions are basic (pH > 7).
explanations.C: NH₃ is a base, not an acid. Its conjugate acid (NH₄⁺) has pKa ≈ 9.25, but NH₃ itself acts as a proton acceptor. A pH of 2.9 would indicate an acidic solution, which contradicts NH₃'s basic nature.
explanations.D: NH₃ is a weak base, not a strong base. Strong bases (NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂) dissociate completely. NH₃ establishes an equilibrium with only partial protonation. A 0.10 M strong base would give pH ≈ 13, but NH₃ yields pH ≈ 11.1.

questions.stem: The conjugate acid of HPO₄²⁻ is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: PO₄³⁻, formed by removing a proton from HPO₄²⁻

choices.label: B
choices.text: OH⁻, because the conjugate acid of any base is hydroxide

choices.label: C
choices.text: H₃PO₄, because the conjugate acid of any phosphate species is phosphoric acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: H₂PO₄⁻, formed by adding a proton to HPO₄²⁻

explanations.A: Removing a proton from HPO₄²⁻ gives PO₄³⁻, which is the conjugate base of HPO₄²⁻, not the conjugate acid. Conjugate acid = original species + H⁺; conjugate base = original species − H⁺.
explanations.B: OH⁻ is the conjugate base of water (H₂O), not the conjugate acid of HPO₄²⁻. Conjugate acid-base pairs differ by exactly one proton and must involve the same molecular species.
explanations.C: H₃PO₄ is the fully protonated form of phosphate, but it is not the conjugate acid of HPO₄²⁻. The conjugate acid is formed by adding exactly one proton: HPO₄²⁻ + H⁺ → H₂PO₄⁻. H₃PO₄ would require adding two protons.
explanations.D: A conjugate acid is formed by adding one proton to a species. HPO₄²⁻ + H⁺ → H₂PO₄⁻. Therefore H₂PO₄⁻ is the conjugate acid of HPO₄²⁻. This is a straightforward application of the Brønsted-Lowry definition: an acid is a proton donor, and the conjugate acid of a base is the species formed when that base accepts a proton.

questions.stem: A Lewis acid is defined as a species that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: donates a proton (H⁺) to another species

choices.label: B
choices.text: accepts a proton (H⁺) from another species

choices.label: C
choices.text: accepts an electron pair from another species to form a coordinate covalent bond

choices.label: D
choices.text: donates an electron pair to another species

explanations.A: Donating a proton defines a Brønsted-Lowry acid, not a Lewis acid. The Brønsted-Lowry definition is narrower than the Lewis definition and focuses specifically on proton transfer.
explanations.B: Accepting a proton defines a Brønsted-Lowry base. This is the opposite of a Brønsted-Lowry acid and is unrelated to the Lewis definition.
explanations.C: A Lewis acid is an electron pair acceptor. It has an empty or partially empty orbital that can accept an electron pair from a Lewis base, forming a coordinate (dative) covalent bond. Examples include BF₃, AlCl₃, metal cations (Fe³⁺, Zn²⁺), and H⁺ itself. The Lewis definition is broader than the Brønsted-Lowry definition because it encompasses reactions that do not involve proton transfer.
explanations.D: Donating an electron pair defines a Lewis base, not a Lewis acid. Lewis bases have lone pairs available for donation (e.g., NH₃, H₂O, OH⁻).

questions.passage: A clinical chemist analyzes arterial blood gas results from a patient. The results show: pH = 7.30, pCO₂ = 60 mmHg (normal: 35-45), HCO₃⁻ = 28 mEq/L (normal: 22-26). The chemist notes that CO₂ dissolved in blood forms carbonic acid: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻.
questions.stem: This patient's acid-base status is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Metabolic acidosis — the primary disturbance is a decrease in HCO₃⁻

choices.label: B
choices.text: Respiratory acidosis with partial metabolic compensation — the primary disturbance is elevated pCO₂, and the kidneys have partially compensated by retaining HCO₃⁻

choices.label: C
choices.text: Respiratory alkalosis — the primary disturbance is decreased pCO₂

choices.label: D
choices.text: Metabolic alkalosis — the primary disturbance is an increase in HCO₃⁻

explanations.A: Metabolic acidosis would present with low pH, low HCO₃⁻ (the primary disturbance), and compensatory low pCO₂ (respiratory compensation through hyperventilation). This patient has high pCO₂ and slightly elevated HCO₃⁻, which is the opposite pattern.
explanations.B: The patient has low pH (7.30 < 7.35, indicating acidosis) and elevated pCO₂ (60 > 45, indicating respiratory origin). In respiratory acidosis, CO₂ retention increases H₂CO₃ and H⁺ production, lowering pH. The slightly elevated HCO₃⁻ (28 > 26) reflects partial renal compensation: the kidneys retain additional bicarbonate to buffer the excess acid. Compensation brings the pH toward normal but does not fully correct it (pH is still acidotic).
explanations.C: Respiratory alkalosis would present with high pH, low pCO₂ (from hyperventilation), and compensatory low HCO₃⁻. This patient has high pCO₂ and low pH — the opposite of alkalosis.
explanations.D: Metabolic alkalosis would present with high pH, high HCO₃⁻ (the primary disturbance), and compensatory high pCO₂ (respiratory compensation through hypoventilation). While this patient has slightly elevated HCO₃⁻ and high pCO₂, the pH is low (acidotic), not high — ruling out alkalosis. The elevated HCO₃⁻ is compensatory, not primary.

questions.stem: The bicarbonate buffer system in blood (CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻) is effective at maintaining blood pH because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: carbonic acid is a strong acid that completely dissociates, ensuring a constant [H⁺]

choices.label: B
choices.text: the system is open — the lungs can rapidly adjust pCO₂ and the kidneys can adjust HCO₃⁻, providing two independent mechanisms for regulating each component of the buffer

choices.label: C
choices.text: the pKa of carbonic acid (6.1) is exactly equal to blood pH (7.4)

choices.label: D
choices.text: blood contains no other buffer systems, so bicarbonate must handle all acid-base regulation alone

explanations.A: Carbonic acid is a weak acid (pKa ≈ 6.1), not a strong acid. If it dissociated completely, there would be no equilibrium and no buffering capacity.
explanations.B: The bicarbonate buffer's effectiveness in vivo is primarily due to its open nature. In a closed system, a buffer with pKa 6.1 would be a poor buffer at pH 7.4 (more than 1 unit away). However, the lungs can rapidly exhale CO₂ (adjusting the acid component) and the kidneys can retain or excrete HCO₃⁻ (adjusting the base component). This dual organ regulation makes the system far more effective than a simple closed-system buffer.
explanations.C: The pKa of carbonic acid (6.1) is more than 1 unit below blood pH (7.4). In a closed system, this would make bicarbonate a poor buffer at physiological pH. The system's effectiveness comes from its open nature (lung and kidney regulation), not from pKa-pH proximity.
explanations.D: Blood contains multiple buffer systems: bicarbonate (primary extracellular), hemoglobin (major intracellular/RBC buffer), plasma proteins, and the phosphate buffer system. Bicarbonate is the most important extracellular buffer, but it does not work alone.

questions.stem: An amino acid in solution at a pH below its isoelectric point (pI) will have a net charge that is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: negative, because the amino group is deprotonated and the carboxyl group is ionized

choices.label: B
choices.text: zero, because all amino acids are zwitterions at any pH

choices.label: C
choices.text: positive, because at pH < pI, the amino acid has gained protons relative to its isoelectric form, resulting in excess positive charge

choices.label: D
choices.text: impossible to determine without knowing the specific amino acid

explanations.A: At pH below pI, the solution is more acidic than the isoelectric point, meaning there is excess H⁺. The amino acid will have gained protons: the amino group will be protonated (NH₃⁺) and the carboxyl group may be protonated (COOH) or partially ionized. The net charge will be positive, not negative. Negative charge predominates at pH above pI.
explanations.B: Amino acids are zwitterions (zero net charge) only at their isoelectric point. At pH values away from pI, they carry a net charge: positive below pI, negative above pI.
explanations.C: At pH below pI, the solution has more H⁺ than at the isoelectric point. The amino acid gains protons relative to its isoelectric form, shifting toward positive charge. For a simple amino acid: at very low pH, both the amino group (NH₃⁺) and the carboxyl group (COOH) are protonated, giving a net charge of +1. As pH rises toward pI, the carboxyl group deprotonates first (COO⁻), and the molecule becomes a zwitterion at pI.
explanations.D: The direction of the charge relative to pI is predictable for all amino acids: positive below pI, negative above pI. The specific pI value varies, but the rule is universal.

questions.stem: The Ka of acetic acid is 1.8 × 10⁻⁵. The Kb of its conjugate base, acetate ion (CH₃COO⁻), is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1.8 × 10⁻⁵, because Ka and Kb are always equal for a conjugate pair

choices.label: B
choices.text: 5.6 × 10⁻¹⁰, because Ka × Kb = Kw = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴, so Kb = Kw/Ka

choices.label: C
choices.text: 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴, because Kb always equals Kw for conjugate bases of weak acids

choices.label: D
choices.text: 1.8 × 10⁵, because Kb = 1/Ka for conjugate acid-base pairs

explanations.A: Ka and Kb are not equal for a conjugate pair. They are related through Kw: Ka × Kb = Kw. For a weak acid with Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵, the conjugate base has a small Kb, indicating it is a weak base — which makes sense because the conjugate base of a weak acid is itself a weak base.
explanations.B: For any conjugate acid-base pair in water: Ka × Kb = Kw = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C. Therefore Kb = Kw/Ka = (1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴)/(1.8 × 10⁻⁵) = 5.6 × 10⁻¹⁰. This means acetate is a very weak base — it only slightly hydrolyzes water. The inverse relationship between Ka and Kb ensures that a strong acid has an extremely weak conjugate base, and vice versa.
explanations.C: Kb = Kw only if Ka = 1, which would describe an acid with Ka = 1 (a moderately strong acid). For acetic acid with Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵, Kb is much larger than Kw.
explanations.D: Kb = 1/Ka would give 1/(1.8 × 10⁻⁵) = 5.6 × 10⁴, implying acetate is a very strong base. This contradicts chemical reality — acetate is a weak base. The correct relationship is Ka × Kb = Kw, not Ka × Kb = 1.

questions.stem: Water acts as both an acid and a base. This property is called:

choices.label: A
choices.text: amphipathic — the ability to interact with both polar and nonpolar environments

choices.label: B
choices.text: amphoteric — the ability to act as either a proton donor (acid) or proton acceptor (base) depending on the reaction partner

choices.label: C
choices.text: hydrophilic — the ability to attract and dissolve polar solutes

choices.label: D
choices.text: hygroscopic — the ability to absorb moisture from the environment

explanations.A: Amphipathic describes molecules with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions (e.g., phospholipids, detergents). It relates to solubility behavior, not acid-base chemistry.
explanations.B: Amphoteric (or more precisely, amphiprotic) describes substances that can act as either acids or bases by donating or accepting protons. Water can donate a proton to a stronger base (acting as an acid: H₂O → OH⁻ + H⁺) or accept a proton from a stronger acid (acting as a base: H₂O + H⁺ → H₃O⁺). The autoionization of water (2H₂O ⇌ H₃O⁺ + OH⁻) demonstrates both behaviors simultaneously. 'Amphiprotic' specifically refers to proton transfer, while 'amphoteric' is the broader term that also covers Lewis acid-base behavior. Amino acids are also amphiprotic.
explanations.C: Hydrophilic means 'water-loving' and describes molecules or functional groups that interact favorably with water. It is a solubility property, not an acid-base property.
explanations.D: Hygroscopic describes substances that absorb water from the air (e.g., NaOH pellets, silica gel). It is unrelated to acid-base behavior.
